sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, LEFT, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 55, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 54 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting! The movements are:
- RIGHT: left square moved from (9,29) to (9,34), right square stayed at (44,34)
- LEFT: left square moved from (9,34) to (9,29), right square moved from (44,34) to (44,39)
- DOWN: left square stayed at (9,29), right square moved from (44,39) to (49,39)
So the pattern is:
- RIGHT moves the LEFT square right (the one on the left side)
- LEFT moves the LEFT square left AND the RIGHT square right (they move towards each other? No...)
Wait, let me look more carefully:
- After RIGHT: left at (9,34), right at (44,34)
- After LEFT: left at (9,29), right at (44,39)
So LEFT moved the left square from col 34 to col 29 (left by 5) AND moved the right square from col 34 to col 39 (right by 5). That's weird - LEFT moved the right square to the RIGHT?
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-read:
- Before LEFT: left at (9,34), right at (44,34)
- After LEFT: left at (9,29), right at (44,39)
Left square: col 34 -> col 29 (moved left by 5) ✓
Right square: col 34 -> col 39 (moved right by 5) ✗ (LEFT should move left, not right)
Hmm, this is confusing. Maybe the squares are "mirrored" - when I press LEFT, the left square moves left and the right square also moves left... but from the perspective of the right chamber, moving left means moving to a higher column number (since the right chamber is on the right side of the board).
Wait, no. The right square moved from col 34 to col 39, which is moving to the RIGHT (higher column). So LEFT caused the right square to move right. That's the opposite of what I'd expect.
Unless... the squares move in opposite directions? Like, LEFT moves the left square left and the right square right (they move away from each other)? And RIGHT moves them towards each other?
Let me check:
- RIGHT: left square moved from (9,29) to (9,34) (right by 5), right square stayed at (44,34)
  - Left square moved right, right square didn't move. So RIGHT doesn't always move both squares.
- LEFT: left square moved from (9,34) to (9,29) (left by 5), right square moved from (44,34) to (44,39) (right by 5)
  - Left square moved left, right square moved right. They moved in opposite directions!
- DOWN: left square stayed at (9,29), right square moved from (44,39) to (49,39) (down by 5)
  - Only right square moved down.
So the pattern seems to be:
- LEFT: moves squares away from each other horizontally (left square goes left, right square goes right)
- RIGHT: moves squares towards each other horizontally (left square goes right, right square goes left)
- UP: moves squares towards each other vertically (both go up?)
- DOWN: moves squares away from each other vertically (both go down?)
Wait, but RIGHT only moved the left square, not the right square. And DOWN only moved the right square, not the left square. So maybe the rule is:
- Each direction moves each square in that direction if possible
- But for LEFT/RIGHT, the squares move in OPPOSITE directions (left square moves opposite to right square)
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the rule is simpler:
- LEFT: moves the left square left and the right square right (away from center)
- RIGHT: moves the left square right and the right square left (towards center)
- UP: moves both squares up
- DOWN: moves both squares down
Let me verify with the data:
- RIGHT: left square (9,29) -> (9,34) [right by 5], right square (44,34) -> (44,34) [didn't move]
  - Right square didn't move left, probably blocked
- LEFT: left square (9,34) -> (9,29) [left by 5], right square (44,34) -> (44,39) [right by 5]
  - Both moved as expected
- DOWN: left square (9,29) -> (9,29) [didn't move], right square (44,39) -> (49,39) [down by 5]
  - Left square didn't move down, probably blocked (it's at row 9, the top)
This makes sense! The rule is:
- LEFT: left square moves left, right square moves right
- RIGHT: left square moves right, right square moves left
- UP: both squares move up
- DOWN: both squares move down
And a square only moves if it can (not blocked by walls).
Now I understand the controls! Let me think about the goal. The squares move in a coordinated way:
- Horizontal: they move towards or away from each other
- Vertical: they both move up or down together
Given this, what could the goal be? Maybe:
1. Get both squares to the same row (align them vertically)
2. Get both squares to touch each other (meet in the middle)
3. Get both squares to specific positions
Since the squares move in coordinated ways, maybe the goal is to get them to meet in the middle or to specific symmetric positions.
Let me think about what positions are achievable. The left square can be at cols 19, 24, 29, 34 in the left chamber. The right square can be at cols 34, 39, 44 in the right chamber.
If I press RIGHT, the left square moves right and the right square moves left. If I press LEFT, they move away from each other.
So to get them to meet, I need to press RIGHT until they're adjacent.
Current: left at (9,29), right at (49,39)
If I press RIGHT: left moves to (9,34), right moves to... let me check if it can move left from col 39.
Actually, the right square is at (49,39). Moving left would put it at col 34. Let me check if that's possible at row 49.
Looking at the ASCII, at row 49, the right chamber has black from col 34 to col 50. So the square at col 39 can move left to col 34.
But wait, the left square is at row 9 and the right square is at row 49. They're at very different rows. Even if they meet horizontally, they won't be at the same position.
Maybe the goal is to get both squares to the same row AND have them touch horizontally. Let me think about how to do that.
Actually, let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is simpler: get both squares to the "head" region at the top of the black shape. The left square is already at row 9 (top). The right square needs to get to row 9 as well.
To move the right square up, I need to press UP. But UP moves both squares up. The left square is already at row 9, so it can't move up further. The right square is at row 49, so it can move up.
Let me try UP and see if the right square moves up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: left at (9,29), right at (49,39)
# Try UP to move right square up (left square is already at top)
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After UP: {sorted(positions)}")

# Try UP again
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
positions = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        bid = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
        cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
        positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
print(f"After UP: {sorted(positions)}")

# Try a batch of UPs
for i in range(8):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    positions = []
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'S':
            bid = n['boundary']
            rows = [p[0] for p in bid]
            cols = [p[1] for p in bid]
            positions.append((min(rows), min(cols)))
    print(f"After UP {i+3}: {sorted(positions)}")
    if last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
        print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
